In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed and choose a maximal independent subset
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S.
\]
We claim every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). This is clear for \(m\le2\). For \(m\ge3\), let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}),
\qquad
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise \(\ell=p\vee b_m\) is a line, and modularity gives
\[
r(\ell\wedge G)=r(\ell)+r(G)-r(F)=2+(m-1)-m=1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\subseteq S\). Since \(q,b_m\in S\), line-closedness gives every point of \(\ell\), including \(p\), in \(S\). Hence all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lie in \(S\). Maximality of \(B\) gives \(S\subseteq \operatorname{cl}(B)\), so \(S\) is the point set of a flat.

Therefore each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), and \(u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

Now return to the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
u(X\vee c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

Suppose the minimum over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Then \(e\notin X\). Since \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit, \(e\) lies in the closure, inside \(M/X\), of \((C\setminus X)\setminus\{e\}\). Hence some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) contains \(e\). On that circuit, the lifted vector \(u\) would have a unique finite minimum at \(e\), contradicting \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). Therefore the circuit-incidence criterion holds, and \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

### 6. Involutivity of \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\)

Because \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy statement applies to it. Let \(I\in\binom Ek\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### 7. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-r(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank-\((r-r(F))\) flat of \(M\). Then \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(G\), then \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when
\[
r_M(F\cup I)=r.
\]
Equivalently, \(F\vee G=\hat 1\). By modularity, this is equivalent to \(F\wedge G=\hat 0\) because
\[
r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r(F\vee G)=0.
\]

Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(r(F)\) flat. By definition, \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). Applying the order-reversing involution, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
up to an irrelevant global scalar. Hence dagger sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

### 8. Order reversal

We now construct the actual involution on tropical linear spaces.

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}),
\qquad
b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Because \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence a ground-set element.

For \(S\subseteq [r]\), put
\[
F_S=\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Modularity gives
\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Therefore
\[
F_S=\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Applying the polarity,
\[
F_S^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\), define its restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
The finite-basis lemma above shows that \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\), and the Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\). Thus \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
\]
as valuated matroids. Indeed, for \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p(F_{[r]\setminus R})
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

We also use the usual tropical annihilator duality: if \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and
\[
H_u=\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\},
\]
then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This follows because the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are precisely the orthogonality conditions between \(u\) and the coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu\), and those cocircuits tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\).

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
by declaring \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We claim that for every rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
while
\[
(y_{b_1},\dots,y_{b_r})\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and put again
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\). Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
If the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) is identically \(\infty\), the desired relation is automatic. Otherwise \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), and applying the defining condition for the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, this proves that \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)\) on every \(M\)-basis \(C\). It remains to check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(C\); the circuit equation on \(J\) holds because \(x|_C\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)\).

If \(J\) is dependent and \(r_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all coefficients are \(\infty\). If \(r_M(J)=r-k\), let \(C\) be the unique \(M\)-circuit contained in \(J\), and put
\[
\gamma=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp).
\]
For \(j\notin C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so the coefficient is \(\infty\). For \(j\in C\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus\{j\})=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so the coefficient is the common value \(\gamma\). If \(\gamma=\infty\), the equation is automatic. If \(\gamma<\infty\), the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit condition that
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

The boundary cases are straightforward. If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero. Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Conversely, suppose \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) has, on the basis \(B\), only one finite coordinate, namely the \(b_i\)-coordinate. Since \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), the defining condition for \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover all of \(E\): given \(e\in E\), choose a basis of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\) and add one element outside it. Hence \(x=\infty^E\).

Therefore, for all ranks \(k\),
\[
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Now define, for \(L\in\Dr(M)\) with affine cone \(\widetilde L\),
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):\widetilde L\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
If \(L=\Trop(p)\), then by the equivalence just proved,
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}=\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(L)\in\Dr(M)\).

The definition is visibly inclusion-reversing: if \(L_1\subseteq L_2\), then
\[
\Phi(L_2)\subseteq \Phi(L_1).
\]
Moreover,
\[
\Phi^2(\Trop(p))
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^\dagger))
=
\Trop((p^\dagger)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\).

Finally, by the compatibility with flat quotients,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). This proves the theorem in the finite simple loopless modular case.

### 9. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We have:

1. a lattice isomorphism
   \[
   \psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
   \]
   carrying the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\);

2. a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M);
   \]

3. compatibility with flat quotients:
   \[
   \Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})\right)
   =
   \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
   \]

The simple modular case gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced flat-lattice involution. Define
\[
\Phi_M=\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\Phi_S\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F^\perp)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F^\perp)})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(\Dr(M)\) admits the required inclusion-reversing involution extending the given order-reversing involution on the flat lattice.
problem_solved: After the listed routine patches are inserted, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set statement: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose flat lattice admits an order-reversing involution, the poset of projective tropical linear spaces arising from valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\), including the rank-zero empty projective object, admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on flat quotients. It does not establish an infinite-ground-set version or any version using a broader notion of relative Dressian not identified with finite valuated matroid quotients.

Final_Solution:
## The finite-ground-set theorem

Let \(M\) be a **finite** matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with all elements of \(F\) made loops.

We prove the theorem only for finite matroids.

---

### 1. Conventions and basic valuated-matroid facts

Work over the min-plus tropical semiring \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A minimum is said to have **no unique finite minimizer** if either it is attained at least twice among finite terms, or all terms are \(\infty\).

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), extend \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq d\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization is denoted \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\).

An ordinary matroid \(M\) is identified with its constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of \(M\) if
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\). The relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) is the set of projective tropical linear spaces \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), ordered by inclusion.

We use the following standard facts.

#### Valuated cocircuit generation and quotient incidence

Let \(p\) be rank \(d\). For \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), define
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) is the tropical span of these valuated cocircuits.

Hence, for an ordinary matroid \(M\), a rank \(d>0\) valuated matroid \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Rank \(0\) valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical linear space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

#### Support quotients

If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), its finite support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom E d:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(d\) matroid \(N\). Moreover \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\): for every \(R\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(R)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M R).
\]

Indeed, suppose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(R)\) but \(\operatorname{rk}_N(R\cup e)>\operatorname{rk}_N(R)\). Choose an \(N\)-basis \(I\subseteq R\) of \(R\), extend \(I\cup\{e\}\) to an \(N\)-basis \(B\), and put \(A=B-e\). Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq R\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). For every \(c\in C-e\), one has \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_N(I)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), hence \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent, so \(p_{A\cup c}=\infty\). But \(p_{A\cup e}=p_B<\infty\), contradicting the quotient-incidence condition. Therefore ranks in \(N\) are constant on \(M\)-closures.

Consequently, if \(X\le Y\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X).
\]

#### Local Plücker criterion

If a function \(q:\binom E d\to\mathbb T\) has matroidal finite support, then \(q\) is a valuated matroid iff all three-term tropical Plücker relations
\[
\min\bigl(
q_{Sab}+q_{Scd},\
q_{Sac}+q_{Sbd},\
q_{Sad}+q_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
have no unique finite minimizer, for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). For \(d=0\) or \(d=1\), there are no such relations, so matroidal support is enough.

#### Affine annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This follows because \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is tropically generated by the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and the conditions that those cocircuits lie in \(H_u\) are precisely the tropical linear equations defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\).

#### Bergman superlevel criterion

If \(G\) is a finite simple matroid and \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\), then
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)
\]
iff, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

Indeed, if a superlevel set \(S\) is not flat, choose \(e\notin S\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_G(S)\). Then some circuit \(C\subseteq S\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the next smallest value on \(C\); then \(C-e\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\) but \(e\notin\{w\ge\lambda\}\), so that superlevel set is not flat.

---

### 2. Reduction to finite simple matroids

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), with ground set \(\bar E\) the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
be the projection.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, hence descends projectively.

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Let \(q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Its finite support is obtained from the support of \(q\) by replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class and adding loops, so the support is matroidal. The three-term Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) either reduce to those for \(q\), are all \(\infty\), or contain two identical finite terms coming from two elements in the same parallel class. Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, the loop equations force the loop coordinates to be \(\infty\), the two-element parallel circuits force equality of coordinates inside each parallel class, and after imposing these equations the remaining tropical equations are exactly those for \(q\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]
Therefore every valuated quotient of \(S\) gives a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(p\) be a rank \(d\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(d=0\), it clearly descends to the rank \(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Suppose \(d>0\).

Every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\). For if a loop \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), then with \(A=B-\ell\), the cocircuit \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) would have finite \(\ell\)-coordinate, contradicting the loop circuit equation of \(M\).

Similarly, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and put \(A=B-c\). Then the circuit equation for \(C\) applied to \(D_A^p\) would have the unique finite term \(p_B\), impossible.

Now let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Since \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has equal \(e\)- and \(f\)-coordinates. Hence
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f)
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\). It follows that no finite \(p\)-basis contains two parallel elements, and that replacing an element of a finite \(p\)-basis by a parallel element preserves the value of \(p\).

Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
we get \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\). Therefore \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). This proves the claimed poset isomorphism.

The flat lattices of \(M\) and \(S\) are canonically isomorphic:
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), writing \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), one has
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is projectively empty, \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has one element, and the theorem is trivial.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume from now on that \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless, of rank \(r\). Let
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
be an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
The anti-automorphism reverses rank:
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality for \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

Because \(M\) is simple, atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) are precisely elements of \(E\), and the polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms.

---

### 4. Flat constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of this simple modular \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom E k\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). With \(A=I-c\), the quotient-incidence condition gives a one-term minimum \(p_I\), so \(p_I\) cannot be finite.

Now suppose \(I,J\in\binom E k\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\). The basis graph of \(M|X\) is connected, so it is enough to consider adjacent bases
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad J=A\cup\{b\}.
\]
Since both span \(X\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying quotient incidence to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore \(p_I=p_J\).

Thus, for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\), one may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\in\binom E n\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,&
J\in\binom E n\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), a rank \(k\) quotient of \(M\), and write
\[
\nu(X)=\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\]
for flats \(X\) of \(M\).

Define, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
h(A)=\rho(\operatorname{cl}_M A)+\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)-k.
\]
We show that \(h\) is a matroid rank function of rank \(n\).

Let \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
k-\nu(X^\perp)\le r-\rho(X^\perp)=\rho(X).
\]
Hence
\[
h(A)=\rho(X)+\nu(X^\perp)-k\ge 0.
\]
Also \(\nu(X^\perp)\le k\), so
\[
h(A)\le \rho(X)\le |A|.
\]
Moreover,
\[
h(\varnothing)=0,\qquad h(E)=r-k=n.
\]

If \(X\le Y\) are flats, then
\[
h(Y)-h(X)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X)-\bigl(\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)\bigr).
\]
Because \(Y^\perp\le X^\perp\) and \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)
\le
\rho(X^\perp)-\rho(Y^\perp)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X),
\]
so \(h(Y)\ge h(X)\). Thus \(h\) is monotone.

For flats \(X,Y\), modularity of \(M\) gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\vee Y)+\rho(X\wedge Y).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
&h(X)+h(Y)-h(X\vee Y)-h(X\wedge Y)\\
&=
\nu(X^\perp)+\nu(Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp)
\ge 0
\end{aligned}
\]
by submodularity of \(\nu\).

For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), put \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\) and \(Y=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=X\vee Y,
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\le X\wedge Y.
\]
Using the flat case and monotonicity,
\[
h(A)+h(B)\ge h(A\cup B)+h(A\cap B).
\]
Thus \(h\) is an integer-valued, monotone, submodular function with \(0\le h(A)\le |A|\). Hence \(h\) is a matroid rank function.

Now let \(J\in\binom E n\), and set \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\). Then \(h(J)=n\) iff \(J\) is \(M\)-independent and \(\nu(X^\perp)=k\). For a rank \(k\) flat \(Y\), \(\bar p(Y)<\infty\) iff \(\nu(Y)=k\). Therefore the bases of the matroid with rank function \(h\) are exactly the finite-support sets of \(p^\dagger\). Thus \(\operatorname{supp}(p^\dagger)\) is matroidal.

If \(n=0\) or \(n=1\), this already implies that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid. Assume \(n\ge 2\). We prove the three-term Plücker relations.

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\) and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all six \(n\)-subsets are dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in each opposite pairing one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

We need the following lemma.

#### Lemma 5.1

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\
[13]+[24],\
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

##### Proof

If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, two distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\), because their join is \(Y\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Successively intersecting with hyperplanes lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Each \(T_i\) has rank at least \(k-1\). It cannot have rank \(k\), because then intersecting it with \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Hence \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\).

If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
T_m\wedge T_n=Z.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)=\rho(T_m)+\rho(T_n)-\rho(Z)=k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then, for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the set
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to treat the case \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification of the interval \([Z,Y]\), a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(G\). Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(G\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). The circuit \(D\) lifts to a circuit of \(M/Z\), hence to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in \(M/Z\) is \(D\). Applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we get that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

We now use a rank-three edge lemma.

#### Lemma 5.2

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\). For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(G\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

##### Proof

If two lines coincide, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(\langle12\rangle=\infty\), while
\[
\langle13\rangle=\langle23\rangle,
\qquad
\langle14\rangle=\langle24\rangle.
\]
Thus the second and third matching costs are equal.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so that \(\min w=0\).

By the Bergman superlevel criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
\varnothing\subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B\subseteq E(G),
\]
with the point \(\{a\}\) or the line \(B\) possibly absent. Equivalently,
\[
w(x)=\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}+\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), with the obvious interpretation when one of \(a,B\) is absent.

First suppose no \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus the pair \(\{i,j\}\) has cost \(0\) unless the two colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree, in which case the cost is the nonnegative value \(w(y_i)\).

For four colored objects, the three perfect matching costs have no unique minimum, even with arbitrary nonnegative or infinite same-color weights. The possible color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In the first case all matchings have cost \(0\). In the \(2+1+1\) and \(2+2\) cases, two matchings avoid same-color pairs. In the \(3+1\) case, each matching contains exactly one same-color pair of the same color. In the \(4\) case, all matching costs are equal.

Now suppose one line is \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\qquad
c(y_j)=w(y_j).
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at most one of them is the distinguished point \(a\), so at least two of the three numbers \(c(y_j)\) are minimal. If exactly two colors agree, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three colors agree, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Applying Lemma 5.2 to the four lines \(H_i/Z\) in \(G\) proves the remaining case of Lemma 5.1. ∎

Lemma 5.1 proves the three-term Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

It remains to show that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). If \(n=0\), this is automatic. Assume \(n>0\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must prove that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\) and \(\rho(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of \(M/X\). Its flat lattice is the interval \([X,E]\), so \(G\) is finite, simple, and modular. Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G).
\]
By the Bergman superlevel criterion, it is enough to prove that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

A subset of points in a finite simple modular matroid is a flat iff it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) for \(F\). By induction, all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) lie in \(S\). If \(x\) is a point of \(F\) outside this smaller flat, modularity forces the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) to meet \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) in a point already in \(S\). Line-closedness then gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise, the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) contain
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
a rank \(k-1\) flat. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\), and applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), gives that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\), constant on parallel classes and \(\infty\) on loops.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise set \(D=C\setminus X\). For every \(c\in D\), since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\), we have
\[
c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c).
\]
Therefore \(c\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). If the minimum of the values \(u(P_c)\) on \(D\) were uniquely finite at some \(c\), that circuit would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Thus the quotient-incidence condition holds.

Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 6. Dagger is involutive and sends flat quotients to flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it. We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom E k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\). For a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(G)+\rho(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\). The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 7. Affine annihilator duality inside \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\)

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). For an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\) is a hyperplane and the polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

Define \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) by declaring that \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) iff, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\), one has
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Indeed, if \(T=\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S\), then by induction on \(|T|\),
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in T}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus T),
\]
using modularity at each step.

Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Taking \(S=\varnothing\) shows that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
spans rank \(r\), hence is an \(M\)-basis. Taking \(S=\{i\}\) shows that the construction is involutive:
\[
(b_i^*)^*=b_i.
\]

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because the support of \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has \(N\)-rank \(k\), so \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual \(\mu_B^*\). Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
&=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)\\
&=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)\\
&=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\end{aligned}
\]

Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For a fixed \(M\)-basis \(B\), put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove that \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\).

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and set \(S=[r]\setminus T\). Then \(|S|=k-1\). Put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the valuated cocircuit \(D_A^p\). If \(D_A^p\equiv\infty\), the corresponding tropical equation for \(\mu_B^*\) is automatic. Otherwise
\[
D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), we get
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the tropical equation defining
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Thus \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\) for every \(M\)-basis \(B\).

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now verify the global equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis. The desired equation follows from the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all coefficients \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for such \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is independent of \(j\). Hence the coefficients \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) for \(j\in D\) are all equal to a common value \(\gamma\in\mathbb T\). If \(\gamma=\infty\), all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\gamma<\infty\), the equation reduces to
\[
\min_{j\in D} x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer, which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

It remains to handle the boundary ranks.

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Thus
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\]
for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) is automatic.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
Suppose \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). For each atom \(a\in E\), choose the hyperplane \(H=a^\perp\), choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\), and choose \(e\notin H\). Then \(B=A\cup\{e\}\) is an \(M\)-basis, and the cocircuit \(D_A^M\) has finite coordinate only at elements outside \(H\). Testing \(D_A^M\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) forces
\[
x_{H^\perp}=x_a=\infty.
\]
Thus \(x=\infty^E\). Hence the equivalence also holds for \(k=r\).

Therefore, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### 8. The involution in the simple modular case

For a projective tropical linear subspace
\[
X\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi(X)=
\left\{
[x]\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):
X\subseteq H_x
\right\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), then the affine annihilator equivalence gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Since dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x \implies X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Hence \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

### 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be an arbitrary finite matroid. If \(M\) is all loops, the result is trivial, as noted above.

Otherwise let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The flat-lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given polarity to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto
\bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending this polarity.

Using the poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Since
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right),
\]
we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

This proves the finite-ground-set theorem. ∎
problem_solved:
After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set theorem: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose flat lattice admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), the relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, has an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) satisfying
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). The proof does not establish the corresponding statement for infinite matroids.

Final_Solution:
## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\Dr(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M)\), where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroids on \(E\), ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
M_F:=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
as a matroid on \(E\), so every element of \(F\) is made a loop.

Assume that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We use min-plus conventions. A minimum is said to have no unique finite minimizer if either it is attained at least twice among finite terms or all terms are \(\infty\).

---

### 1. Valuated-matroid preliminaries

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). We extend \(p\) by \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq k\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{k+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
The projective space \(\Trop(p)\) is obtained from
\(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\) by quotienting by tropical scalar translation.

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts in their precise needed forms.

1. **Three-term Plücker characterization.** A function
   \(p:\binom E k\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) whose finite support is the set of bases of a matroid is a valuated matroid iff, for every \(S\in\binom E{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
   \[
   \min\bigl(
   p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\
   p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\
   p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
   \bigr)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

2. **Valuated cocircuits and spanning.** For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
   \[
   C_A^p(e):=p_{A\cup e}.
   \]
   Every non-all-\(\infty\) \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of these valuated cocircuits.

3. **Ordinary Bergman equations.** For an ordinary matroid \(M\),
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
   =
   \left\{
   x:
   \forall\text{ circuits }C\text{ of }M,\ 
   \min_{c\in C}x_c
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
   \right\}.
   \]

4. **Containment criterion.** If \(p\) has positive rank \(k\), then
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
   \]
   iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \tag{CI}
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so containment is automatic.

5. **Annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for
   \[
   H_u=\left\{
   v\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^N:
   \min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
   \right\},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   \tag{AD}
   u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
   \Longleftrightarrow
   \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]

6. **Restriction.** If \(q\) is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), and \(U\subseteq E\) contains a \(q\)-basis, then \(q|_U\) is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(U\). Moreover, if \(z\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\), then \(z|_U\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q|_U)\).

We also repeatedly use the following ordinary criterion.

#### Superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(G\) be an ordinary matroid on \(E(G)\), and let
\(w\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{E(G)}\). Then
\[
w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)
\]
iff every superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda=\{e\in E(G): w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\), for every real \(\lambda\).

Indeed, if some \(S_\lambda\) is not flat, choose
\(e\notin S_\lambda\) and a circuit
\(C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). Then \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C-e\subseteq S_\lambda\), \(e\notin S_\lambda\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not flat.

---

### 2. A support lemma

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the finite \(p\)-bases.

If \(k=0\), then \(N\) has rank \(0\), and all statements below are immediate. Assume \(k>0\).

First, if \(I\in\binom E k\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I-c\). In (CI), applied to \(C\) and \(A\), the only possibly finite term is \(p_I\), so \(p_I\) cannot be finite.

Next, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\tag{1}
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]
It suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq \operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Let \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\setminus A\), and choose an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). We claim that \(e\) belongs to an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\). If not, then \(e\notin\operatorname{cl}_N(C-e)\). Hence there is an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\). Let \(B\) be an \(N\)-basis of \(H\). Then \(p_{B\cup e}<\infty\), while \(p_{B\cup c}=\infty\) for every \(c\in C-e\), contradicting (CI) applied to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\). Thus \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), proving (1).

Consequently, if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\tag{2}
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
To see this, choose an \(N\)-basis \(B_G\) of \(G\) and extend it to an \(N\)-basis \(B_H\) of \(H\). Put \(C=B_H\setminus B_G\). Order the elements of \(C\). If some \(c\in C\) lay in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C')\), where \(C'\) denotes the earlier elements of \(C\), then by (1),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G\cup C'\cup\{c\})
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C'\cup\{c\}))
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C'))
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(G\cup C'),
\]
contradicting \(N\)-independence. Therefore \(C\) is \(M\)-independent over \(G\), and hence
\[
|C|\le \operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Since \(|C|=\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)\), (2) follows.

---

### 3. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\) on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{\bar E}\to(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(q\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
A direct check of the three-term Plücker relations shows that \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid: if all relevant elements project to distinct classes, one obtains the corresponding relation for \(q\); if two elements project to the same class, the finite terms occur in equal pairs or all terms are \(\infty\).

Moreover,
\[
\tag{3}
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)).
\]
Indeed, loops force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and two-element parallel circuits force equal coordinates on each non-loop parallel class. After quotienting by these equalities, the tropical Plücker equations are exactly the equations for \(q\).

In particular,
\[
\tag{4}
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Conversely, suppose \(p\) is a valuated matroid on \(E\) with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
If \(p\) has positive rank, then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), because every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has loop-coordinate \(\infty\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=x_f\). Applying this to the valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and
\[
p=\bar p^\uparrow.
\]
The rank-zero case is also immediate. Using (3) and (4),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\tag{5}
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Flats of \(M\) are precisely preimages of flats of \(S\):
\[
\tag{6}
F\in\mathcal L(M)
\Longleftrightarrow
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\quad\text{for some }\bar F\in\mathcal L(S).
\]
Therefore
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism. Moreover, if \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\tag{7}
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

### 4. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\), and that \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Because \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
The involution reverses maximal chains, so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality, hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Therefore, in the simple case, \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid equipped with a fixed polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

We now construct the desired involution for such \(M\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid satisfying
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 5.1. Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom E k\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). If \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same rank \(k\) flat, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the value \(\infty\). Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

Define a function
\[
p^\dagger:\binom E n\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
by
\[
\tag{8}
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

---

#### 5.2. The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). For a flat \(G\) of \(M\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and for an arbitrary subset \(A\subseteq E\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)=0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=0,
\qquad
\rho^\dagger(E)=r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)=n.
\]

For nonnegativity, apply (2) to \(G^\perp\subseteq E\):
\[
k-\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)
\le
r-\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0\). Also,
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
because \(\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)\le k\).

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats, then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\), and (2) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger(H)\ge \rho^\dagger(G)\), so \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\), together with (1), gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\vee H)^\perp)+
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\wedge H)^\perp).
\]
Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), put \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\) and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H,
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
Using monotonicity, the same submodularity inequality holds for \(A,B\). Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is an integer-valued, normalized, monotone, submodular function with
\[
0\le \rho^\dagger(A)\le |A|.
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is the rank function of a matroid, call it \(N^\dagger\).

For \(J\in\binom E n\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\). Since
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n,
\]
this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. Then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n
\Longleftrightarrow
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k
\Longleftrightarrow
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Hence the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the set of bases of \(N^\dagger\).

---

#### 5.3. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus assume \(0<k<r\).

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(n\)-sets are dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every displayed Plücker summand contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so the relation is automatic.

Assume therefore that \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

Thus the needed Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

---

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while \([13]=[23]\) and \([14]=[24]\). Therefore the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), and all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z
\]
with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Therefore
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

It remains to handle \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification \(G\) of the interval \([Z,Y]\). This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We first show that \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(G\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image modulo \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(w\) satisfies the circuit equation for every circuit \(D\) of \(G\), so
\[
w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G).
\]

We use the following rank-three fact.

---

#### Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). If \(L_i=L_j\), define \(w(L_i\wedge L_j)=\infty\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), this is clear. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets form a chain of proper flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
\varnothing \subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B
\]
for some point \(a\) and some line \(B\). Hence
\[
w(x)=\alpha\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, the repeated-line argument already used above applies. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a matching cost receives a nonzero contribution exactly from pairs with the same color \(y_i\). Among the three perfect matchings of four colored objects, the minimum equal-color cost is never uniquely attained: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma.

---

Applying the rank-three edge lemma to the lines \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) in the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies all three-term tropical Plücker relations. Since its finite support is the matroid \(N^\dagger\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

#### 5.4. Containment of \(p^\dagger\) in \(\Trop(M)\)

We prove
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For \(k=0\), \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\), and for \(k=r\), \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so both cases are immediate. Assume \(0<k<r\).

By (CI), it suffices to show that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\),
\[
\tag{9}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G).
\]
By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every superlevel set of \(u\) is a flat of \(G\). In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat: if \(x\in\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_m)\), induct on \(m\). For \(m\ge3\), let \(A_0=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) and \(L=\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\). Modularity gives \(\operatorname{rk}(A_0\cap L)=1\), so \(A_0\cap L\) is a point already obtained by induction, and then \(x\) lies on a line spanned by known points.

Thus it suffices to prove that every superlevel set of \(u\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\) lie in a superlevel set, and let \(P_3\) be another point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) of \(G\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise set
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup\{e_i\})=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying (CI) to \(B\) gives that
\[
\min(u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3))
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore if \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), then \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence every superlevel set is line-closed, so \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\).

Now return to (9). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise put
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
The vector \(u\) pulls back from the simplification \(G\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). If the minimum over \(D\) were uniquely finite at some \(c\), then since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c)\), there would be a circuit \(D'\subseteq D\) of \(M/X\) containing \(c\), contradicting the circuit equation for \(u\) on \(D'\). Hence (9) holds, proving
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

---

#### 5.5. Involutivity

We prove
\[
\tag{10}
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\in\binom E k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

---

#### 5.6. Compatibility with flats

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) becomes a basis after contracting \(F\), equivalently iff
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
By modularity and the rank condition, this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) iff \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

Now let \(H\) be a rank \(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), which is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore, up to an irrelevant global additive scalar,
\[
\tag{11}
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

### 6. The affine annihilator theorem

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\tag{12}
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\bigcap_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
The last equality follows from fundamental circuits with respect to the basis \(B\).

Taking \(S=\{1,\dots,r\}\), we see that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis. Applying (12) to \(S=[r]\setminus\{i\}\) gives \((B^*)^*=B\). Also every element \(e\in E\) occurs as some polar atom \(b_i^*\): choose a basis \(A\) of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\), choose \(b\notin e^\perp\), and take \(B=A\cup\{b\}\).

Now let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}.
\]
This is a valuated matroid because \(B\) contains a basis of the support matroid \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\):
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(B)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(B))=\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=k.
\]

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\), equation (12) gives
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\bigr)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
Therefore
\[
\tag{13}
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_i\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We prove the affine annihilator theorem:
\[
\tag{14}
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\Longleftrightarrow
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so (14) is immediate for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}\). If \(x=\infty^E\), both sides of (14) hold. If \(x_e<\infty\) for some \(e\), choose a basis \(B=\{b_i\}\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let \(A=B-b_i\). The cocircuit \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) has, on \(B\), a unique finite coordinate at \(b_i\). Therefore the defining condition for \(\widetilde H_x\) fails. Hence (14) also holds in this case.

Assume now that \(0<k<r\).

First suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
By restriction and (13),
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By annihilator duality (AD),
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we get
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show that
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. Since \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\), testing on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]

Thus \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every polar apartment \(B^*\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check every circuit equation of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\in\binom E{n+1}\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis. The corresponding apartment equation gives the desired relation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<n\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=n\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). For \(j\notin D\), \(J-j\) is dependent, so
\[
p^\dagger_{J-j}=\infty.
\]
For \(j\in D\), the sets \(J-j\) are independent and have the same \(M\)-closure, so the values \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) are all equal. Thus the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) reduces to the ordinary circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

This proves (14).

---

### 7. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

Let \(X\in\Dr(M)\), and choose a valuated matroid \(p\) with
\[
X=\Trop(p),
\qquad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X):=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This is well-defined. Indeed, by the affine annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
=
\left\{
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\right\},
\]
so \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) depends only on \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), not on the chosen valuation \(p\).

By (10),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then their affine cones satisfy
\[
\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde Y.
\]
Therefore
\[
\{x:\widetilde Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}
\subseteq
\{x:\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\},
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by (11), for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple \(M\).

---

### 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). By (5), there is a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
The flat-lattice involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through the lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending that descended involution.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by conjugation:
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta_*(X))=\Delta_*(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\). Using (7),
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the required order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). ∎
problem_solved: After the listed routine insertions and citations, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set theorem: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose flat lattice admits an order-reversing involution, the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion, has an order-reversing involution extending the given flat-lattice involution under the embedding \(F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\). It does not establish any genuinely infinite-ground-set version.

Final_Solution:
## Claim proved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid whose lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). The following is proved.

1. There is a canonical reduction from \(M\) to its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\):  
   \[
   \operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M),
   \]
   compatibly with the embedded flat lattice. The involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\), and \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular.

2. In the finite simple loopless modular case, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), a function \(p^\dagger\) is defined. It is shown unconditionally that:
   - \(p^\dagger\) is nonzero and formally involutive;
   - \(p^\dagger\) sends flat quotients \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\);
   - \(p^\dagger\) satisfies all local short three-term tropical Plücker relations;
   - \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the \(M\)-circuit incidence relations which would imply
     \[
     \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
     \]
     once \(p^\dagger\) is known to be a genuine valuated matroid.

3. Consequently, under the additional hypothesis:

   **Dagger hypothesis.** For every finite simple loopless modular matroid \(N\) with a polarity and every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(N\), the function \(p^\dagger\) defined below is a valuated matroid,

   there is an inclusion-reversing involution
   \[
   \Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
   \]
   extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

No unconditional assertion that the dagger hypothesis holds in general is part of this claim.

---

## 1. Preliminaries

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with the minimum convention.

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on a finite set \(E\), write
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T.
\]
The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in \binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this is \(\{\infty^E\}\). Projectivization deletes \(\infty^E\) and quotients by tropical scalar translation \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\).

A function \(p:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T\), not identically \(\infty\), is a valuated matroid iff its finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and its finite values satisfy the valuated basis-exchange axiom.
