Equivalently, \(p\) satisfies the full tropical Plücker relations. The short three-term relations alone are not used here as a valuated-matroid criterion.

For an ordinary matroid \(M\), we regard \(M\) as the valuated matroid with value \(0\) on \(M\)-bases and \(\infty\) otherwise.

We use the following standard facts.

### Fact 1.1: Quotient-containment criterion

Let \(p\) be a rank \(d>0\) valuated matroid on \(E\). Then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
iff for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), the minimum
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, where
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(d=0\), containment is automatic.

When this condition holds, \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of \(M\).

### Fact 1.2: Coordinate cocircuits

If \(p\) has rank \(d>0\), then for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p).
\]

### Fact 1.3: Affine annihilator duality

If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), then for \(u\in\mathbb T^N\),
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\]
iff
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)
\subseteq
\left\{
v\in \mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Fact 1.4: Uniqueness

A valuated matroid is determined by its affine tropical linear space up to adding a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates.

We also need the following elementary description of ordinary Bergman fans.

### Lemma 1.5: Superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(N\) be an ordinary matroid on a finite ground set \(E_N\). For \(w\in\mathbb T^{E_N}\),
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
\]
iff, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda(w)=\{e\in E_N:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

#### Proof

Suppose \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). If \(S_\lambda(w)\) is not flat, then there is \(e\notin S_\lambda(w)\) with
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w)).
\]
Hence some circuit \(C\subseteq S_\lambda(w)\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\). Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the minimum of \(w\) on \(C\) is uniquely attained at \(e\), contradiction.

Conversely, suppose all superlevel sets are flats. If some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the other finite values on \(C\), and larger than \(w_e\) if all other values are \(\infty\). Then
\[
C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda(w),
\qquad
e\notin S_\lambda(w),
\]
but
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda(w)),
\]
contradicting flatness. ∎

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple loopless matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(\bar q\) is a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q\) to \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The full tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) are precisely those for \(\bar q\), together with tautological relations involving loops or parallel representatives. Hence \(q\) is again a valuated matroid, and
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
If \(q\) has rank \(0\), then it is the zero-parallel extension of the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(q)=d>0\).

For every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), Fact 1.2 gives
\[
D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Thus \(D_A^q\) satisfies every \(M\)-circuit equation.

First, no finite \(q\)-basis contains an \(M\)-circuit. Indeed, if \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(C\subseteq I\) is an \(M\)-circuit, choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
D_A^q(c)=q_I<\infty,
\]
while \(D_A^q(e)=\infty\) for every \(e\in C\setminus\{c\}\), contradicting the circuit equation for \(C\).

In particular, no finite \(q\)-basis contains a loop of \(M\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f).
\]
This implies both that no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same parallel class and that replacing one representative of a parallel class by another preserves the value of \(q\).

Therefore \(q\) descends to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\): for \(\bar I\in\binom{\bar E}{d}\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
This is independent of representatives, and the Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow from those of \(q\). Moreover \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\).

Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]

Hence \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Now compare flats. A subset \(F\subseteq E\) is a flat of \(M\) iff
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a unique flat \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\). Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). The matroid
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
has rank function
\[
r(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Since
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
this matroid is precisely the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Therefore \(\Delta_*\) identifies the flat object indexed by \(\bar F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) with the flat object indexed by \(F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\) by
\[
\bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]

---

## 3. A polarity forces modularity after simplification

Let \(L\) be a finite geometric lattice of rank \(r\), with rank function \(\rho\), and suppose \(L\) has an order-reversing involution \(X\mapsto X^\perp\).

Since \(X\mapsto X^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism, it identifies the interval \([\hat 0,X]\) with \([X^\perp,\hat 1]\). Hence
\[
\rho(X^\perp)=r-\rho(X).
\]

Geometric lattices are semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Applying this to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\), and using
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
gives
\[
r-\rho(X)+r-\rho(Y)
\ge
r-\rho(X\vee Y)+r-\rho(X\wedge Y).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\). Therefore \(L\) is modular.

Thus, after passing to the simplification, the relevant case is:

- \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless;
- \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular;
- \(M\) has rank \(r\);
- \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is a fixed order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

---

## 4. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), and equipped with a polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\):
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]

### Lemma 4.1: Flat constancy

Assume \(k>0\). For every rank \(k\) flat \(F\) of \(M\), the value \(p_I\) is independent of the \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

#### Proof

Every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. Indeed, if a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I<\infty,
\]
while \(D_A^p(e)=\infty\) for all \(e\in C\setminus\{c\}\), contradicting the quotient-containment criterion.

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(A\) and \(C\), the only possibly finite terms are
\[
p_{A\cup a}
\quad\text{and}\quad
p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore they are equal in \(\mathbb T\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected, so any two \(M\)-bases of \(F\) are connected by such exchanges. Hence \(p_I\) is constant over all \(M\)-bases of \(F\). ∎

### Lemma 4.2: Finite \(p\)-bases inside \(M\)-bases

Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

#### Proof

Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). By Lemma 4.1, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, so it extends to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\).

Let \(B\) be any \(M\)-basis. Since the basis graph of \(M\) is connected, it suffices to pass across one exchange
\[
B''=B'\setminus\{b\}\cup\{e\}.
\]
Assume \(B'\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I\), set
\[
A=I\setminus\{b\}.
\]
Let \(C\subseteq B'\cup\{e\}\) be the fundamental circuit containing \(b\). Because \(B''\) is a basis, this circuit also contains \(e\).

Since
\[
D_A^p(b)=p_I<\infty,
\]
the circuit equation for \(C\) forces another finite term
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_{A\cup c}<\infty
\]
with
\[
c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\}).
\]
Then \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. ∎

We now define \(p^\dagger\).

If \(k=0\), then \(p\) has a single coordinate \(p_\varnothing\). Define \(p^\dagger\) to be the ordinary valuated matroid \(M\), shifted by \(p_\varnothing\).

If \(k=r\), then Lemmas 4.1 and 4.2 imply that \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\). If its finite basis value is \(c\), define \(p^\dagger\) to be the rank-zero valuated matroid with coordinate \(c\).

Assume now
\[
0<k<r,
\qquad
n=r-k.
\]
For \(J\in\binom En\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

At this stage \(p^\dagger\) is only a function \(\binom En\to\mathbb T\). Its valuated-matroid status is exactly the dagger hypothesis.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is nonzero. Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

Also, \(p^\dagger\) is formally involutive: applying the same formula again gives \(p\). Indeed, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and the formal \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I,
\]
where \(J\) is any \(M\)-basis of \(F^\perp\).

---

## 5. Local short three-term relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the local short three-term tropical Plücker relations. This does not imply that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid; it is only one verified local property.

### Lemma 5.1: Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two lines coincide, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(\langle12\rangle=\infty\), while
\[
\langle13\rangle=\langle23\rangle,
\qquad
\langle14\rangle=\langle24\rangle.
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w=\infty^E\), all relevant quantities are \(\infty\), and there is nothing to prove. Otherwise subtract a scalar so that \(\min w=0\).

By Lemma 1.5, the positive superlevel sets of \(w\) are nested proper flats of the rank-three matroid \(N\). Hence they are contained in a point-line flag
\[
a\le B.
\]
Thus there exist \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus an edge \(\{i,j\}\) has positive cost only when the two colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. Among four colored objects, the minimum perfect matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are one of
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4,
\]
and in each case at least two perfect matchings attain the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are distinct, at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal the minimum value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three matching costs are equal. ∎

### Lemma 5.2: Local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third matching sums are equal.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\).

Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting a flat with a hyperplane in a modular lattice lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\ge k-2.
\]
Also \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\), so
\[
\rho(Z)\le k.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(\rho(T_i)\ge k-1\), and if \(\rho(T_i)=k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would force \(\rho(Z)\ge k-1\), contradiction.

Moreover
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we have
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z.
\]
Then \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The short three-term Plücker relation for the valuated matroid \(p\), applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\,
[13]+[24],\,
[14]+[23]
\bigr),
\]
so the desired condition holds.

It remains to treat \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We claim that
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose
\[
e_P\in P\setminus Z.
\]
By the standard circuit lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
such that
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}
=
\bar p(P)
=
w(P).
\]
Thus the minimum of \(w\) on \(D\) has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

Each \(H_i\) descends to a line \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) descends to \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Lemma 5.1 therefore gives the desired conclusion. ∎

### Proposition 5.3: Local short three-term relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Assume \(0<k<r\), and let \(n=r-k\). For every
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and every four distinct elements \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\), the minimum
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all three sums are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every matching sum has an \(\infty\)-summand, so the relation is automatic.

Assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\rho(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\rho(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For distinct \(e,f\), the set \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent iff
\[
X\vee e=X\vee f,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
H_e=H_f.
\]
If \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is independent, then
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee e\vee f)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Therefore the desired three-term relation is precisely Lemma 5.2 applied to
\[
H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d.
\]
∎

---

## 6. Incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We next prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the \(M\)-circuit incidence relations.

We use two elementary facts about contractions.

### Lemma 6.1: Circuit lifting for contractions

Let \(X\) be a flat of \(M\).

1. If \(D\) is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and \(e_P\) is a representative in the corresponding parallel class for each \(P\in D\), then there exists an \(M\)-circuit
   \[
   C\subseteq X\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
   \]
   such that
   \[
   C\setminus X=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
   \]

2. If \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), then every \(e\in C\setminus X\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). Equivalently, the parallel class of \(e\) lies in a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) contained in the set of parallel classes met by \(C\setminus X\).

#### Proof

For (1), the chosen representative set is dependent in \(M/X\), and no proper subset meeting fewer simplification classes is dependent, by minimality of \(D\). Hence it is a circuit of \(M/X\). Circuits of a contraction are precisely minimal nonempty sets of the form \(C'\setminus X\), where \(C'\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\). This gives the desired \(M\)-circuit.

For (2), let \(e\in C\setminus X\). Since \(C\) is a circuit,
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{e\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\})\bigr).
\]
Thus \(e\) is dependent on \(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\}\) in \(M/X\). A minimal dependent subset gives a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\). ∎

Intervals of a modular lattice are modular, and simplification preserves the lattice of flats. Thus, for every flat \(X\), the simplification \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) is again a finite simple modular matroid.

### Lemma 6.2: Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank \(n-1\) flat of \(M\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

#### Proof

By Lemma 1.5, it suffices to show that every real superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we show \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be another point on the line spanned by \(P_1,P_2\) in \(N\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp.
\]
Then \(Y\) has rank \(k+1\), and the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Because \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp
\supseteq
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\rho(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose
\[
e_i\in H_i\setminus K.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. By Lemma 6.1, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying the quotient-containment criterion to \(B\) and \(D\), elements of \(D\cap K\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}
=
\bar p(H_i)
=
u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Now, in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Let \(\mathcal S\) be line-closed, and let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal S).
\]
Choose an independent spanning set
\[
b_1,\dots,b_m\in\mathcal S
\]
for \(F\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness.

Assume \(m\ge 3\). Put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), then \(p\in\mathcal S\). Otherwise, consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
Since \(G\vee \ell=F\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(\ell\wedge G)
=
\rho(\ell)+\rho(G)-\rho(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\). Since \(q,b_m\in\mathcal S\), line-closedness implies that every point of the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(\mathcal S\).

Therefore every superlevel set \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat, so Lemma 1.5 gives
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
∎

### Proposition 6.3: Incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Assume \(0<k<r\), and let \(n=r-k\). For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\). For \(c\in X\),
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the flat
\[
P=X\vee c
\]
is a point of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), and
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
By Lemma 6.2, the corresponding function on \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) lies in its Bergman fan.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise, suppose the minimum on \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Then \(e\notin X\). By Lemma 6.1, the parallel class of \(e\) lies in a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) contained in the classes met by \(C\setminus X\). On that circuit, the induced function has a unique finite minimum at the class of \(e\), contradicting Lemma 6.2.

Thus the incidence relation holds for every \(C,A\). ∎

Therefore, if the dagger hypothesis holds so that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid, Proposition 6.3 and Fact 1.1 imply
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]

---

## 7. Flat quotients

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(p^F\) denote the ordinary valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Thus \(p^F\) has rank \(r-\rho(F)\).

A finite basis of \(p^F\) is an \(M\)-basis of a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\) complementary to \(F\), meaning
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(G\vee F=E\) says that \(G\) spans \(M/F\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(G\wedge F)
=
\rho(G)+\rho(F)-\rho(G\vee F)
=
(r-\rho(F))+\rho(F)-r
=
0.
\]

Let \(f=\rho(F)\). The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) has rank \(f\). For a rank \(f\) flat \(H\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on the \(M\)-bases of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is complementary to \(F\). Applying the polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being complementary to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

Thus the dagger construction sends the flat object indexed by \(F\) to the flat object indexed by \(F^\perp\).

---

## 8. Conditional construction of the order-reversing involution

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, and satisfies the dagger hypothesis.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple and \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a single element of \(E\).

For \(S\subseteq [r]\), write
\[
B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]

### Lemma 8.1: The basis-star involution

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is involutive.

#### Proof

First,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}).
\]
The inclusion “\(\le\)” is clear. Conversely, if an element \(e\) is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)\), then either \(e=b_j\) for some \(j\notin S\), in which case \(e\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_j\})\), or else the fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B\) uses some \(b_j\) with \(j\notin S\), again implying
\[
e\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_j\}).
\]
Thus the equality holds.

Applying the polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\bigvee_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]

Taking \(S=\varnothing\), we get
\[
E=\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*),
\]
so \(B^*\) spans. Since \(B^*\) has \(r\) elements in a rank \(r\) simple matroid, it is an \(M\)-basis.

Taking \(S=\{i\}\), we get
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\}).
\]
Applying \(\perp\) again gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i).
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, the star of \(b_i^*\) relative to \(B^*\) is \(b_i\). Hence \(B\mapsto B^*\) is involutive. ∎

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Proposition 8.2: Point-annihilator description

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Under the dagger hypothesis,
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\]
for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

#### Proof

First assume \(0<k<r\), and set \(n=r-k\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
(\mu_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Lemma 4.2 ensures that \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\).

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=n\), Lemma 8.1 gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_{[r]\setminus R})^\perp.
\]
Therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(B_{[r]\setminus R})\bigr)
=
p_{B_{[r]\setminus R}}
=
(\mu_B)_{[r]\setminus R}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\]
and
\[
y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p).
\]
Then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). We prove
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(n+1\), and put
\[
S=[r]\setminus T,
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
Let
\[
A=B_S.
\]
By Fact 1.2,
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p),
\]
so \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}
\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus\{i\}}
=
(\mu_B)_{S\cup\{i\}}
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{n+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since \(x|_C\) lies in the tropical linear space of \(p^\dagger|_C\), the circuit equation for \(J\) holds.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho_M(J)<n\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally, suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho_M(J)=n\). Then \(J\) has nullity \(1\), so it contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\notin C\), the set \(J\setminus\{j\}\) remains dependent. For \(j\in C\), the set \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}
\]
is independent of \(j\in C\). If this common value is \(\infty\), the circuit equation is automatic. Otherwise the equation reduces to the ordinary \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
for \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary cases are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
and \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\), shifted by a scalar. For \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), both sides of the desired equivalence hold.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar shift of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then clearly
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in \widetilde H_x\) on \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite, so
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) varies, the elements \(b_i^*\) range over all of \(E\), because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of bases. Thus \(x=\infty^E\). ∎

We can now construct the involution.

For an affine tropical linear space
\[
X=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
define
\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
By Proposition 8.2,
\[
\widetilde\Phi\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi(X)\) is again an affine tropical linear space contained in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If
\[
X\subseteq Y,
\]
then
\[
Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\implies
X\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so
\[
\widetilde\Phi(Y)\subseteq \widetilde\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\widetilde\Phi\) reverses inclusion.

By formal involutivity of the dagger construction,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]

Finally, Section 7 shows that for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde\Phi\!\left(
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

All constructions commute with tropical scalar translation, so \(\widetilde\Phi\) descends to an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
extending the given polarity on flats.

---

## 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be an arbitrary finite matroid with a polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(M)=0\), then projectively \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has a single object; the identity is the required involution.

Assume \(\operatorname{rk}(M)>0\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
By Section 2,
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat quotients. The polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By Section 3, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular.

If the dagger hypothesis holds for \(S\), Section 8 gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending the descended polarity. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), set
\[
\bar F=\psi(F).
\]
Compatibility of \(\Delta_*\) with flat quotients gives
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The descended polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus, for finite \(M\), the desired order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) is obtained conditionally from the dagger hypothesis, while the reduction to the simple modular case and the stated local and incidence properties of \(p^\dagger\) hold unconditionally.
problem_solved: After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the following conditional finite result: for a finite matroid \(M\) whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution, one may reduce canonically to the simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), whose flat lattice is modular. In the finite simple loopless modular case with polarity, the construction \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is defined for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), is nonzero and formally involutive, sends flat quotients indexed by \(F\) to those indexed by \(F^\perp\), and satisfies the stated local short three-term relations and \(M\)-circuit incidence relations. Assuming the additional dagger hypothesis that every such \(p^\dagger\) is a genuine valuated matroid, the construction gives an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the polarity on flats. The proof does not establish the dagger hypothesis and therefore does not solve the original unconditional problem.

Final_Solution:
## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a matroid on a **finite** ground set \(E\). Suppose that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), including the empty projective rank-zero space, ordered by inclusion. Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_M:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with every element of \(F\) made a loop.

---

### 1. Tropical and valuated-matroid preliminaries

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with min-plus conventions. If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), define its affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) by the tropical Plücker circuit equations
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p)
\iff
\text{for every }J\in \binom{E}{k+1},\ 
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(k=0\), we use the convention
\[
\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
The associated projective tropical linear space is
\[
L(p):=\bigl(\widetilde L(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
Thus a rank-zero valuated matroid gives the empty projective space.

An ordinary matroid \(N\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
N_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ is a basis of }N,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), and \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate valuated cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the standard finite-valuated-matroid cocircuit-generation theorem: every \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), and \(\widetilde L(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the \(D_A^p\).

For \(u\in\mathbb T^R\), set
\[
H_u
=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

If all \(u_i=\infty\), then \(H_u=\mathbb T^R\). Otherwise, let \(\nu\) be the rank-one valuated matroid on \(R\) with \(\nu_{\{i\}}=u_i\). Then \(H_u=\widetilde L(\nu^*)\), because the only rank-\(|R|-1\) circuit equation for \(\nu^*\) is precisely
\[
\min_i(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
Hence \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex.

We need the following annihilator form of tropical duality.

**Lemma 1.1.** Let \(\mu\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\). Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]

**Proof.** If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde L(\mu)=\{\infty^R\}\), while \(\widetilde L(\mu^*)=\mathbb T^R\). Since \(\infty^R\in H_u\) for every \(u\), the statement is immediate.

Assume \(k>0\). The circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) say that for every
\[
J\in\binom{R}{|R|-k+1},
\]
the minimum
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(u_j+\mu^*_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Put \(A=R\setminus J\). Then \(|A|=k-1\), and
\[
\mu^*_{J\setminus j}=\mu_{A\cup j}.
\]
Thus this condition is exactly that \(u\) be tropically orthogonal to the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^\mu\):
\[
\min_{i\in R}\bigl(u_i+D_A^\mu(i)\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
So \(u\in\widetilde L(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu\).

Because \(\widetilde L(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those cocircuits, and because \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex, this is equivalent to
\[
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]
∎

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), the Bergman fan admits the following useful description.

**Lemma 1.2.** Let \(N\) be an ordinary finite matroid, and let \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\). Then
\[
w\in\widetilde L(N)
\]
iff every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\},\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R,
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** The ordinary Bergman condition says that every circuit of \(N\) has no unique finite \(w\)-minimum.

Suppose some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\). Choose \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\) with
\[
w_e<\lambda\le w_c
\quad\text{for all }c\in C\setminus\{e\}.
\]
Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\), but \(e\notin\{w\ge\lambda\}\). Since \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\), this superlevel set is not a flat.

Conversely, suppose \(S=\{w\ge\lambda\}\) is not a flat. Choose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(S)\setminus S\). Then there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq S\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\). ∎

We also use the following containment criterion.

**Lemma 1.3.** Let \(M\) be an ordinary finite matroid, and let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E(M)\). For \(k=0\), one always has \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\). For \(k>0\),
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)
\]
iff for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** For a circuit \(C\) of \(M\), define \(u^C\in\mathbb T^E\) by
\[
u^C_i=
\begin{cases}
0,& i\in C,\\
\infty,& i\notin C.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}H_{u^C}.
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq H_{u^C}
\iff
u^C\in\widetilde L(p^*).
\]
The circuit equations for \(p^*\) are indexed by \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), and the equation corresponding to \(A\) is precisely
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
having no unique finite minimizer. ∎

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of nonloop parallel classes, let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

First, if \(\bar q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q\) to \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is obtained from \(\bar q\) by repeatedly adding loops and zero-valued parallel copies. These two single-element operations preserve valuated matroids: a loop extension sets every basis containing the new element to \(\infty\), and a zero-parallel extension of \(e\) by \(f\) sets bases containing \(f\) but not \(e\) equal to the corresponding bases with \(e\), while bases containing both \(e\) and \(f\) have value \(\infty\). The tropical Plücker relations reduce immediately to the old ones after deleting loops or identifying parallel elements.

The corresponding tropical linear space is exactly
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Indeed, a loop extension forces the new coordinate to be \(\infty\). If \(f\) is added parallel to \(e\), then the equation on \(A\cup\{e,f\}\), where \(A\cup e\) is a finite basis if \(e\) is not a loop, forces \(x_e=x_f\); if \(e\) is already a loop, then both coordinates are forced to be \(\infty\). After imposing these equalities and loop coordinates, the remaining equations are precisely those of \(\bar q\).

Applying this to the ordinary matroid \(S\) gives
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)).
\]
Thus every valuated quotient of \(S\) extends to a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), then \(q\) descends uniquely to the rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

If \(e\in L_0\), then \(\{e\}\) is a circuit of \(M\). Lemma 1.3 implies that for every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\) with \(e\notin A\),
\[
q_{A\cup e}=\infty.
\]
So every loop of \(M\) remains a loop for \(q\).

If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit. Lemma 1.3 gives:
- if \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f};
  \]
- if \(e\in A\) and \(f\notin A\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup f}=\infty,
  \]
  and similarly with \(e\) and \(f\) exchanged.

Therefore no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same parallel class, and the value of a finite basis is independent of the choice of representatives.

Finally, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is dependent in \(M\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then in the condition of Lemma 1.3 for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(q_I\). Hence \(q_I=\infty\). Thus every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.

Consequently \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those of \(q\), and \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\). Since
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q))
\quad\text{and}\quad
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)),
\]
the inclusion \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\) implies
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq\widetilde L(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Therefore \(\Delta_*\) is a bijection on relative Dressians and preserves inclusions.

The flats of \(M\) are precisely inverse images of flats of \(S\). Explicitly,
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then the rank formula
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
\]
shows that \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite and simple.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is finite and simple of rank \(r\). If \(r=0\), then \(E=\varnothing\), and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) consists only of the rank-zero projective space. The identity map is the required involution.

Assume \(r>0\). The lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Since \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution, it sends maximal chains to maximal chains, so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using order reversal and rank reversal, this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Hence \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

---

### 4. Flat constancy for valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), meaning
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]

First, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is dependent in \(M\), then
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]
Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), choose \(c\in C\), and put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). In Lemma 1.3, the only possibly finite term for \(C,A\) is \(p_I\), so \(p_I=\infty\).

Second, if \(I\) and \(I'\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]
It suffices to check adjacent bases of \(M|F\). Suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\},
\]
with \(|A|=k-1\). Since both sets are bases of \(F\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Lemma 1.3 applied to \(C,A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected, so the claim follows.

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)
\]
to be the common value of \(p_I\) over all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\). This value may be \(\infty\).

We also need one more fact: every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I_0\). Since \(I_0\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any other \(M\)-basis. The basis graph of \(M\) is connected, so move from \(B_0\) to \(B\) by single exchanges. Suppose \(B'=B-b+e\) is adjacent to \(B\), and \(I\subseteq B\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B'\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{b\}\). Let \(C\) be the fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B\). Since \(B-b+e\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). In Lemma 1.3 for \(C,A\), the term \(p_I\) is finite, so there must be some
\[
c\in C\setminus A,\qquad c\neq b,
\]
with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Since \(c\in B'\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B'\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Induction along the path in the basis graph proves the claim.

---

### 5. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(n\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero. Hence those boundary cases are immediate.

Assume for the moment that
\[
0<k<r.
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) has at least one finite basis: choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Since \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-k=n\),
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I
<\infty.
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

#### 5.1. Line-closed sets in modular matroids

**Lemma 5.1.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(A\) is a set of points of \(N\) such that whenever \(x,y\in A\), every point on the line \(x\vee y\) also lies in \(A\). Then \(A\) is the set of points of a flat.

**Proof.** Let
\[
F=\bigvee A.
\]
We show that every point below \(F\) lies in \(A\). Since \(N\) is finite, it suffices to prove this for a flat generated by finitely many independent points of \(A\). We argue by induction on the rank \(d\) of such a flat.

The claim is clear for \(d=0,1,2\), with \(d=2\) being exactly the line-closure assumption. Assume \(d\ge3\), and write
\[
F=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_d
\]
with \(a_i\in A\) independent. Let
\[
G=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{d-1}.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(A\). Let \(x\le F\) be a point. If \(x\le G\) or \(x=a_d\), then \(x\in A\). Otherwise, the line \(x\vee a_d\) is not contained in \(G\). By modularity inside the interval \([\hat0,F]\),
\[
\rho((x\vee a_d)\wedge G)
=
\rho(x\vee a_d)+\rho(G)-\rho(F)
=
2+(d-1)-d
=
1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=(x\vee a_d)\wedge G
\]
is a point of \(G\), hence \(y\in A\). Since \(x\) lies on the line \(a_d\vee y\), line-closure gives \(x\in A\). ∎

#### 5.2. A rank-three matching lemma

**Lemma 5.2.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let \(w\in\widetilde L(N)\), and let \(\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3,\ell_4\) be lines of \(N\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(\ell_i\wedge \ell_j),&\ell_i\neq \ell_j,\\
\infty,&\ell_i=\ell_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two of the \(\ell_i\) coincide, then one matching sum has an \(\infty\)-term and the other two matching sums are equal. So assume the \(\ell_i\) are distinct.

If \(w=\infty^E\), the result is immediate. Otherwise subtract the minimum finite value of \(w\). By Lemma 1.2, the superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank \(3\), every proper chain is contained in a flag
\[
P\le L,
\]
where \(P\) is a point and \(L\) is a line. Thus
\[
w=\alpha\mathbf 1_L+\beta\mathbf 1_P
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb T\) with \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), where \(\mathbf 1_F\) denotes the function that is \(1\) on \(F\) and \(0\) outside, interpreted additively.

First suppose no \(\ell_i\) equals \(L\). Put
\[
q_i=\ell_i\wedge L.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the point \(\ell_i\wedge\ell_j\) lies on \(L\) exactly when \(q_i=q_j\). If \(q_i=q_j\), its contribution is
\[
\gamma(q_i)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&q_i=P,\\
\alpha,&q_i\neq P.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_i\neq q_j\), the contribution is \(0\). The equality pattern of four labels has type
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
The corresponding matching-sum patterns are, up to permutation,
\[
(0,0,0),\quad (\gamma,0,0),\quad (\gamma+\delta,0,0),\quad
(\gamma,\gamma,\gamma),\quad (2\gamma,2\gamma,2\gamma),
\]
with \(\gamma,\delta\ge0\). None has a unique finite minimum.

Now suppose one line, say \(\ell_1\), equals \(L\). Put
\[
q_i=\ell_i\wedge L,\qquad i=2,3,4.
\]
Then
\[
[1i]=\gamma(q_i),
\]
and for \(i,j\in\{2,3,4\}\),
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\gamma(q_i),&q_i=q_j,\\
0,&q_i\neq q_j.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_2,q_3,q_4\) are all distinct, then the three matching sums are
\[
\gamma(q_2),\gamma(q_3),\gamma(q_4).
\]
At most one of these is \(\alpha+\beta\), and the others are \(\alpha\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two labels agree, the pattern is
\[
(\gamma,\gamma,\gamma+\delta),
\]
up to permutation. If all three agree, all three sums are equal. ∎

#### 5.3. A local dual Plücker lemma

**Lemma 5.3.** Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two \(H_i\)'s coincide, the claim follows as in Lemma 5.2. Assume they are distinct, and put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular, intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most \(1\), hence
\[
\rho(Z)\ge k-2.
\]
Also \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and for distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\),
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k.
\]
Thus
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), choose an atom \(Q_i\) of this interval such that
\[
Q_i\le \bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j,
\qquad
Q_i\not\le H_i.
\]
This is possible because \(\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j\) strictly contains \(Z\), while its meet with \(H_i\) is \(Z\).

The atoms \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4\) are independent over \(Z\): indeed, \(Q_i\) is not contained in the join of the other three, because that join lies inside \(H_i\), whereas \(Q_i\not\le H_i\).

Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
h_i\in Q_i\setminus Z.
\]
For \(\{i,j,\ell,m\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z\vee Q_\ell\vee Q_m.
\]
Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup h_\ell\cup h_m}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on
\[
A,h_1,h_2,h_3,h_4.
\]

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(Y/Z),
\]
a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). Define a weight on the points \(P\) of \(N\) by
\[
w(P)=\bar p(Z\vee P).
\]
This is well-defined by flat constancy. The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\). It has value \(\infty\) on \(Z\), and its value is constant on each point of \(Y/Z\), equal to \(w(P)\). By Lemma 1.2, the superlevel sets of \(D_A^p\) are flats of \(M\); intersecting with \(Y\) and passing to \(Y/Z\) shows that the superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats of \(N\). Therefore
\[
w\in\widetilde L(N).
\]
The hyperplanes \(H_i\) descend to lines \(\ell_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and
\[
[ij]=w(\ell_i\wedge \ell_j).
\]
The claim follows from Lemma 5.2. ∎

#### 5.4. A codimension-one polarity lemma

**Lemma 5.4.** Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1\), and let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde L(N).
\]

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.2, it suffices to show that every superlevel set
\[
\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is finite simple modular, Lemma 5.1 reduces this to showing line-closure.

Let \(P_1,P_2\) be points of \(N\) with
\[
u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda,
\]
and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
W=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\rho(W)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(W\), and choose
\[
h_i\in H_i\setminus W.
\]
In the contraction \(M/W\), the three elements \(h_1,h_2,h_3\) represent three distinct points on a rank-two line. Hence \(\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) is a circuit in \(M/W\). Equivalently,
\[
A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}
\]
is dependent in \(M\), while every
\[
A\cup\{h_i,h_j\}
\]
with \(i\neq j\) is independent. Therefore any circuit contained in \(A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) contains all three \(h_i\).

Applying Lemma 1.3 to such a circuit and to \(A\), we get that
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup h_1},p_{A\cup h_2},p_{A\cup h_3}\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
p_{A\cup h_i}=\bar p(H_i)=\bar p(P_i^\perp)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence every superlevel set is line-closed, and therefore a flat of \(N\). ∎

#### 5.5. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient

We verify the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient-incidence relations.

If \(n<2\), there are no Plücker relations to check. Assume \(n\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is dependent in \(M\), then every \(n\)-set appearing in the Plücker relation is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent. If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.

Thus assume none of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
S\cup\{e,f\}
\]
is independent iff \(H_e\neq H_f\), and in that case
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is dependent, and the corresponding value is \(\infty\). Hence the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly Lemma 5.3.

Now we check quotient-incidence. Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup c\) is dependent. For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By Lemma 5.4, the function
\[
X\vee c\longmapsto \bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in
\[
\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X)).
\]

Suppose the minimum over \(c\in C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). In \(M/X\), the point \(X\vee c_0\) lies in the closure of the points \(X\vee c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{c_0\}\). Hence in \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) there is a circuit containing \(X\vee c_0\) and contained in the set of points \(X\vee c\) with \(c\in C\setminus X\). On this circuit, the above function would have a unique finite minimum at \(X\vee c_0\), contradicting membership in the Bergman fan. Thus the quotient-incidence relations hold.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 6. Involutivity and flat-quotient compatibility

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is dependent in \(M\), then both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is an involution on valuated quotients.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank-\(r-\rho(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), iff
\[
F\vee G=E.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(F)+\rho(G)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\hat0.
\]
Thus finite bases of \(p^F\) correspond exactly to lattice complements of \(F\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\), since
\[
(G\vee F)^\perp=G^\perp\wedge F^\perp,
\qquad
(G\wedge F)^\perp=G^\perp\vee F^\perp.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 7. Order reversal

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a single ground-set element.

We first record the dual-basis identity. For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

The containment “\(\le\)” is immediate: if \(i\in S\), then
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S)\le \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\}),
\]
so
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S)^\perp.
\]

It remains to prove equality. We show that the \(b_i^*\) are independent. Suppose \(j\notin T\) and
\[
b_j^*\le \bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*.
\]
Applying \(\perp\) gives
\[
\left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp
\le
(b_j^*)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_j\}).
\]
But from the containment already proved,
\[
\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T)^\perp,
\]
so
\[
\left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp
\ge
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T).
\]
Since \(j\notin T\), we have \(b_j\in B\setminus T\), hence
\[
b_j\in \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T)
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_j\}),
\]
contradicting independence of \(B\). Therefore the \(b_i^*\) are independent. Both sides of the displayed identity have rank \(|S|\), so equality holds.

In particular,
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde L(M):
\text{for every }M\text{-basis }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We now prove the key annihilator characterization.

**Proposition 7.1.** Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x .
\]

**Proof.** First assume \(0<k<r\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has at least one finite basis.

For \(|T|=r-k\), the dual-basis identity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in T\}}
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin T\}}
=
\mu_B^*(T).
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose first that
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger),
\qquad
y\in\widetilde L(p).
\]
Then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B).
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\). For every \(A\subseteq B\) with \(|A|=k-1\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), we find that \(x|_{B^*}\) is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*).
\]
Thus \(x\) satisfies every \(p^\dagger\)-equation supported inside every basis \(B^*\).

Now let
\[
J\subseteq E,
\qquad
|J|=r-k+1.
\]
We check the \(p^\dagger\)-equation for \(J\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since every \(M\)-basis is of the form \(B^*\) for another \(M\)-basis \(B\), this equation has already been checked.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is dependent, then every coefficient in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation is \(\infty\). Otherwise \(J\) has rank \(|J|-1\), hence contains a unique circuit \(C_0\). The only finite coefficients can occur for \(j\in C_0\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus\{j\})=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Thus the \(p^\dagger\)-equation for \(J\) is either trivial or reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) on the circuit \(C_0\). Hence
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary ranks are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Since \(\infty^E\in\widetilde H_x\) for every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), the equivalence is true.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
We must show that
\[
\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x
\]
holds iff \(x=\infty^E\).

If \(x=\infty^E\), this is immediate. Conversely, suppose \(x\neq\infty^E\), and choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Let
\[
H=e^\perp,
\]
a hyperplane of \(M\). Choose an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_1\})=H.
\]
Then \(b_1^*=e\). Choose \(A\gg0\), and define
\[
y_f=
\begin{cases}
A,& f\in H,\\
0,& f\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
The superlevel sets of \(y\) are flats, so \(y\in\widetilde L(M)\). For \(A\) sufficiently large,
\[
y_{b_1}+x_{b_1^*}=x_e
\]
is the unique finite minimum among the numbers
\[
y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Thus \(y\notin\widetilde H_x\). Hence \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) forces \(x=\infty^E\). ∎

It follows immediately that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses inclusions. Indeed, if
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(q),
\]
and \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\), then Proposition 7.1 gives
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by Proposition 7.1,
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Moreover, Proposition 7.1 gives the intrinsic description
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\}.
\]
Therefore the construction depends only on the tropical linear space \(\widetilde L(p)\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
the map
\[
L(p)\longmapsto L(p^\dagger)
\]
defines an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The construction is compatible with projectivization because adding a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf 1\) to \(x\) or \(y\) does not change the condition defining \(\widetilde H_x\). Rank-zero affine spaces projectivize to the empty space, which is included in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, by the flat-quotient computation above,
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\) of the finite simple modular matroid \(M\).

---

### 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be any finite matroid whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given involution to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). If \(S\) has rank \(0\), then \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\), and hence \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), has only the rank-zero projective space, and the identity map is the desired involution. Otherwise, the simple case applies to \(S\), giving an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Using the simplification isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
This is an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). We already showed that
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The transported polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the required inclusion-reversing involution on the relative Dressian of the finite matroid \(M\), extending the prescribed order-reversing involution on the embedded flat lattice. ∎
problem_solved: After inserting the listed routine patches and standard references, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set theorem: for every finite matroid \(M\) whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution, the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), with the empty rank-zero projective space included, has an inclusion-reversing involution extending the embedded flat-lattice involution. It does not establish an infinite-ground-set version.
